ULCC/VLCC100k–565k250–4400.82–0.8513–15.75

Four Cornerstones of Stability
KM = KG + GM = KB + BM

∙ KB — Centre of Buoyancy: depends on underwater hull geometry
∙ BM — Metacentric Radius: BM = I/V
∙ KG — Centre of Gravity: depends on loading
∙ GM — Metacentric Height: measure of initial stability
Box: KB = d/2
Tri: KB = 2d/3
Ship: KB ≈ 0.535d
Box: BM = B²/12d
Tri: BM = B²/6d
Ship: BM ≈ Cw²B²/12dCb
⚠ Minimum GM at all times: 0.15 m (includes free surface effects)
Typical GM Values by Ship Type
General Cargo
0.30–0.50 m
Oil Tanker / VLCC
0.30–1.00 m
Container / RoRo
~1.50 m
Bulk-Ore Carrier
2.0–3.0 m

Knowledge Hub — Stability Theory
10 in-depth stability theory modules covering Ship Dimensions, Archimedes & Buoyancy, GM, Loll, GZ Curve, Trim, FSE, Simpson's Rules, Ship Squat and Form Coefficients — a structured reference for naval architecture calculations and stability assessment.
Stability Theory
Stability Theory
This section provides a structured technical reference for the core stability principles used in naval architecture and ship operations. It combines practical definitions, formulae, visual explanations and calculation context across 10 modules: Ship Dimensions, Archimedes & Buoyancy, Metacentric Height (GM), Angle of Loll vs List, GZ Curve, Trim, Free Surface Effect, Simpson's Rules, Ship Squat and Form Coefficients.
📏
Ship Dimensions
LOA, LWL, LBP, Breadth, Depth, Draft & Freeboard — the principal measurements that define a ship's hull form.
⚓
Archimedes & Buoyancy
Every floating body displaces its own weight of liquid. Foundation of all stability theory.
📐
Metacentric Height (GM)
The most critical single parameter in ship stability. GM determines initial restoring ability.
〰
Angle of Loll vs. List
Two distinct phenomena often confused. Understanding the difference is safety-critical.
📊
Statical Stability Curve
GZ vs angle of heel — the complete picture of a vessel's stability across all angles.
⇄
Trim & Longitudinal Stability
How ships change drafts when weights are loaded, discharged, or shifted fore and aft.
💧
Free Surface Effect
Liquid in slack tanks causes a virtual rise of G, reducing effective GM and stability.
🏗
Simpson's Rules
Numerical integration for areas, volumes, centroids and moments of inertia of ship forms.
⬇
Ship Squat
Reduction in underkeel clearance caused by ship's own speed in restricted waters.
⬡
Form Coefficients
Cw, Cb, Cm and Cp — definitions, formulas, SVG diagrams and ship-type reference tables from Barras & Derrett Ch. 9.
▸ Form Coefficient Definitions
Cw
Waterplane Area Coefficient
COEFFICIENT OF FINENESS OF WATER-PLANE
The ratio of the area of the waterplane to the area of a circumscribing rectangle of the same length and maximum breadth.
Cw = Area of Waterplane ÷ (L × B)
∴ WPA = L × B × Cw
Approx: Cw = (⅔ × Cb) + ⅓  (at mld draft only)
Shaded area = ship waterplane; ABCD = circumscribing rectangle. Cw is always < 1.0.
Cb
Block Coefficient
COEFFICIENT OF FINENESS OF DISPLACEMENT
The ratio of the volume of displacement at a given draft to the volume of a circumscribing rectangular block having the same overall length, breadth and depth (draft).
Cb = Vol. of Displacement ÷ (L × B × d)
∴ Vol. = L × B × d × Cb
Lifeboat (wooden plank): Cb = 0.6 (standard value)
Shaded = ship displacement volume; dashed = circumscribing block (L × B × d).
Cm
Midships Coefficient
MIDSHIP AREA COEFFICIENT
The ratio of the transverse area of the midships section (Am) at a given draft to the area of a circumscribing rectangle of the same breadth and depth.
Cm = Am ÷ (B × d)
∴ Midships Area Am = B × d × Cm
Merchant ships: Cm = 0.980 – 0.990 at fully loaded draft
Shaded = midship section area (Am); dashed = rectangle (B × d). Bilge radius reduces Cm below 1.0.
Cp
Prismatic Coefficient
LONGITUDINAL PRISMATIC COEFFICIENT
The ratio of the volume of displacement to the volume of a prism of the same length as the ship and the same cross-section as the ship's midships area (Am). Used primarily by ship-model researchers.
Cp = Vol. of Displacement ÷ (L × Am)
∴ Volume = L × Am × Cp
Key: Cb = Cm × Cp  ∴  Cp = Cb ÷ Cm
Shaded = ship displacement vol.; dashed prism = L × Am. Cp is always slightly higher than Cb.
Key Relationships & Rules of Thumb
Fundamental Identity
Cb = Cm × Cp
∴ Cp = Cb ÷ Cm
Cw Approximation
Cw ≈ (⅔ × Cb) + ⅓
Valid at moulded draft only
WPA from Cw
WPA = L × B × Cw
TPCSW = WPA ÷ 97.56
Volume from Cb
V = L × B × d × Cb
W = V × ρ (t/m³)
Draft Variation
Cw ≈ Cb + 0.100
Curves are parallel through all drafts
All Coefficients
0 < Cw, Cb, Cm, Cp < 1
None can ever equal or exceed unity
Typical Cb Values at Fully Loaded Draft  
Ship Type Cb (fully loaded) Hull Form Typical Cw Typical Cp (Cm=0.985)
ULCC0.850Full-form0.9000.863
Supertanker0.825Full-form0.8830.838
Oil Tanker0.800Full-form0.8670.812
Bulk Carrier0.775 – 0.825Full-form0.850 – 0.8830.787 – 0.838
General Cargo Ship0.700Medium-form0.8000.711
Passenger Liner0.575 – 0.625Fine-form0.717 – 0.7500.584 – 0.635
Container Ship0.575Fine-form0.7170.584
Coastal Tug0.500Fine-form0.6670.508
ℹ️  Hull form classification: Full-form: Cb > 0.700  |  Medium-form: Cb ≈ 0.700  |  Fine-form: Cb < 0.700  ·  Merchant ships Cm = 0.980–0.990 at fully loaded draft. Derived Cw uses Cw = (⅔ × Cb) + ⅓. Cp = Cb ÷ 0.985.
Variation of Cb and Cw with Draft  — Fig 9.5, Barras & Derrett

Both Cb and Cw decrease as the ship rises from fully loaded draft towards lightship. For a typical general cargo ship (135.5 m LBP) the values at fully loaded draft are Cb = 0.701 and Cw = 0.801. At lightship draft they reduce to Cb = 0.613 and Cw = 0.713. The two curves run parallel throughout all drafts, with a constant offset of approximately 0.100. A constant “K” is calculated at the fully loaded condition and held fixed for all remaining drafts down to lightship.

FULLY LOADED
Cb = 0.701
Cw = 0.801
LIGHT BALLAST
Cb ≈ 0.650
Cw ≈ 0.750
LIGHTSHIP
Cb = 0.613
Cw = 0.713
Offset Cw − Cb ≈ 0.100 is maintained at every draft from fully loaded to lightship (general cargo ship, 135.5 m LBP).

Density Change
FWA Calculator
Trim Calculation
Results
Change of Draft — Density Change (Box-shape)
Results
Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)
Formula: FWA = W / (4 × TPCSW)
The FWA is added when loading in fresh water, and deducted when loading in denser water than salt water.
Results

Free Surface Effect
FSE — Loss of GM Calculator
Calculates the virtual loss of GM due to free surfaces in slack tanks. Always reduce FSE to a minimum.
FSE Results
Drydocking
Drydocking — Upthrust & Loss of GM
Calculate upthrust P and resulting loss of GM at the critical moment when stern takes the blocks, or when water is pumped out.
Results
Rolling Period
Natural Rolling Period Calculator
Result
Rolling Period Reference:
Tender ship: TR = 30–35 sec
Comfortable ship: TR = 20–25 sec
Stiff ship: TR = 8–10 sec

Risk: If ship's period approaches wave period → Synchronism → potential capsizing. Alter course or speed.
Ship Squat
Maximum Squat Calculator
Squat is the reduction in UKC caused by ship motion. Critical in ports, rivers, and shallow channels.
Squat Results
Speed vs Squat Reference Table (for entered Cb)
Holtrop–Mennen Resistance
Calm-Water Resistance & Power Calculator
Holtrop–Mennen (1982/1984) statistical regression method. Estimates frictional, wave-making and correlation-allowance resistance, and effective power, from principal hull-form parameters. Assumes no bulbous bow and no immersed transom.
Resistance & Power Results
Speed vs Resistance & Power Table
USER GUIDE
NavStab · Naval Architecture Platform
Overview What this platform contains
Naval Architecture Platform
NavStab combines a professional Calculators Suite with a dedicated Knowledge Hub — Stability Theory. The User Guide popup now lists all main dashboard options, what each option is used for, and the recommended way to navigate through the platform.
Dashboard Main Options
Calculators Suite
Opens the practical calculation tools used for hull form, transverse stability, drafts, displacement, free surface correction, drydocking checks, rolling period estimation, ship squat assessment, and Holtrop–Mennen resistance / effective power prediction.
Knowledge Hub — Stability Theory
Opens the theory library for stability and naval architecture fundamentals, including buoyancy, GM, GZ curve, trim, free surface effect, Simpson's Rules, squat, and form coefficients.
Calculators Suite All Calculator Details
1. Design Coefficients
Use this calculator for principal hull-form coefficients such as block coefficient, prismatic coefficient, waterplane coefficient, and midship coefficient. It supports quick comparison of hull fullness and form efficiency.
2. Transverse Stability
Use this option to check transverse stability parameters such as KB, BM, KM, KG, GM, and basic stability condition. It is useful for understanding initial stability and margin against instability.
3. Trim & Drafts
Use this tool to evaluate trim, forward draft, aft draft, mean draft, and related longitudinal balance checks. It helps review how loading changes affect vessel attitude.
4. TPC & Displacement
Use this calculator to estimate tonnes per centimetre immersion and displacement-related values. It supports draft change checks and quick hydrostatic approximations.
5. Free Surface Effect
Use this option to calculate free surface correction and understand the reduction in effective GM due to slack tanks or liquid movement within tanks.
6. Drydocking
Use this calculator for docking-related stability checks, including support reaction and stability considerations as the vessel takes blocks during drydocking.
7. Rolling Period
Use this tool to estimate rolling period and relate it to vessel beam, GM, and rolling behaviour. It helps provide a practical indication of stiffness or tenderness.
8. Ship Squat
Use this calculator to assess squat effect in shallow or restricted waters. It supports under-keel clearance awareness and speed-related squat review.
9. Holtrop–Mennen Resistance
Opens the dedicated Holtrop–Mennen Resistance calculator for calm-water resistance and effective power prediction from hull-form inputs. The module supports review of frictional resistance (RF), wave-making resistance (RW), total resistance (RT), form factor (1+k1), and effective power PE for early-stage powering assessment.
Holtrop–Mennen Resistance Calculator Guide
Resistance & Power Prediction
Holtrop–Mennen Resistance opens the attached professional resistance calculator from the NavStab dashboard. Use it when the vessel hull-form particulars are available and a calm-water resistance / effective power prediction is required.
Primary Output
Total calm-water resistance and effective power PE from ship principal particulars and hull-form coefficients.
Resistance Components
Review RF, RW and RT contributions so the user can understand frictional, wave-making and total resistance behaviour.
Form Factor
Includes the form factor (1+k1), supporting evaluation of viscous resistance allowance linked with hull fullness.
Speed–Power Review
Supports speed-power assessment for early design comparison and powering trend review from the calculated resistance values.
Navigation: From the NavStab landing page, select Calculators Suite, then open Holtrop–Mennen Resistance. In the Holtrop–Mennen page, the top Back button returns to the NavStab landing page.
Knowledge Hub Stability Theory Module Details
1. Ship Dimensions
Explains principal dimensions such as length, breadth, depth, draft, freeboard, and their role in ship design and stability checks.
2. Archimedes & Buoyancy
Covers buoyant force, displacement, centre of buoyancy, and the fundamental principle that supports vessel flotation.
3. Metacentric Height (GM)
Explains the relationship between KG, KM, and GM, and why GM is important for initial transverse stability.
4. Angle of Loll vs List
Clarifies the difference between an external heeling/list condition and an unstable loll condition caused by negative or very low GM.
5. GZ Curve
Explains righting lever, righting moment, range of stability, maximum GZ, and how the GZ curve is interpreted.
6. Trim
Covers longitudinal stability, change of trim, LCF, MCTC, and draft changes caused by shifting weights or loading changes.
7. Free Surface Effect
Explains how liquid movement in slack tanks reduces effective GM and why free surface correction is required.
8. Simpson's Rules
Explains approximate area, volume, and moment calculations used in hydrostatics and naval architecture calculations.
9. Ship Squat
Explains sinkage and trim change due to speed in shallow/restricted water and its importance for under-keel clearance.
10. Form Coefficients
Explains block, prismatic, waterplane, and midship coefficients and how they describe hull shape and fullness.
Workflow Recommended Use
1
Open Dashboard
Start from the main NavStab dashboard and select the required calculator or theory module.
2
Enter Inputs
Fill in ship-specific values using the units shown beside each input field.
3
Calculate / Review
Use the calculator action button or read the theory module details for the selected topic.
4
Navigate Back
Use Back, Home, or Back to Dashboard to return to the main platform screen.
Navigation Header Buttons
← Back Button
Returns to the previous browser page. If no previous page is available, it returns to the dashboard.
⌂ Home Button
Returns directly to the main NavStab dashboard page inside the platform.
User Guide Button
Opens this popup window containing complete platform option details, calculator details, and Knowledge Hub theory module details.
Back to Dashboard
Available inside calculator pages to return from the selected calculator back to the main dashboard.
Note Engineering Use
Important: This tool is intended for technical calculation support, training, and quick reference. Always verify final vessel stability, loading, drydocking, squat, and operational decisions against approved ship documents, class requirements, and applicable regulations.
